walkthrough.title: Choose Player Colors
walkthrough.text: Let's get Through the Ages set up. Each player chooses a color and takes the player board in that color. Place your player board on the table in front of you, and leave plenty of space around it for your civilization to grow. This space around your board is called your player area.

walkthrough.title: Collect Player Pieces
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a set of 7 wooden pieces in their color. You get two octagonal points counters for tracking culture and science points, four round rating markers for culture, science, strength, and happiness, and one tactics standard for marking your current military tactic. If this is your first game, you can decorate them with the included stickers.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Blue Bank
walkthrough.text: Take 16 blue transparent cubes and place them on the squares of your blue bank on your player board. Blue tokens represent food and resources. Fill the bank completely from left to right. When sections of your blue bank are empty later in the game, you will face corruption, so keep an eye on this bank throughout.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Yellow Bank and Workers
walkthrough.text: Take 25 yellow transparent cubes. Place 18 in your yellow bank on your player board. Place 6 on your starting technology cards: 1 on Warriors, 2 on Agriculture, 2 on Bronze, and 1 on Philosophy. Place the last yellow cube in your worker pool. These 7 yellow tokens outside the yellow bank are your starting workers. The one in your worker pool is an unused worker, available to be put to work.

walkthrough.title: Place Action Tokens
walkthrough.text: Take 4 white tokens and 2 red tokens and place them next to the right edge of your player board. White tokens represent civil actions and red tokens represent military actions. Your starting government, Despotism, printed on your board provides 4 civil actions and 2 military actions. These tokens will move on and off your government card as you spend and reset actions.

walkthrough.title: Set Happiness Marker
walkthrough.text: Put your happiness rating marker on space 0 of the happiness indicator on your player board. At the start of the game, only Religion could provide happy faces, but you have no temples yet, so your happiness is zero. As your population grows, you will need to increase happiness to avoid discontent workers and uprisings.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Card Decks
walkthrough.text: Separate all cards into eight decks by their backs: Age A, Age I, Age II, and Age III, each split into civil and military cards. For a 2-player game, remove cards marked 3+ and 4+ from Age I and II civil decks. For a 3-player game, remove only cards marked 4+. For your first game, return both Age III decks to the box and limit the Age I and II military decks to events, territories, tactics, and military bonus cards only. Shuffle each deck separately.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Card Row
walkthrough.text: The card row is the central element of the game. It has 13 spaces where civil cards are displayed for players to draft. Deal 13 cards from the Age A civil deck face up to fill the card row. The cost to take a card depends on its position: 1 civil action for the first five cards, 2 for the next four, and 3 for the rightmost four. Place the remaining Age A civil deck on the current age board.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Science Board
walkthrough.text: Place the science board where everyone can see it. All players place their round science rating markers on space 1 of the science rating indicator. You start with 1 science rating because your lab on Philosophy produces 1 science. All players place their octagonal science points counters on space 0 of the science points track. You start with no accumulated science points.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Culture Board
walkthrough.text: Place the culture board where everyone can see it. All players place their round culture rating markers on space 0 of the culture rating indicator. You have no temples yet, so you produce no culture. All players place their octagonal culture points counters on space 0 of the culture points track. Culture points determine the winner at the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Military Board
walkthrough.text: Place the military board centrally. All players place their strength rating markers on space 1 of the strength indicator. You start with 1 warrior, which gives strength 1. Place all players' tactics standards in the common tactics area on the military board. No one has a current tactic yet. The military board also has spaces for the future events deck, current events deck, and past events pile.

walkthrough.title: Create the Current Events Deck
walkthrough.text: Take the top cards from the Age A military deck equal to the number of players plus two. So for 2 players take 4 cards, for 3 players take 5, for 4 players take 6. Place them face down on the current events space of the military board. These are mostly positive events that will be revealed during play. Return the rest of the Age A military deck to the box without looking at the cards. The future events space starts empty.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Current Age Board
walkthrough.text: The current age board holds the civil and military decks for the current age. After dealing cards to the card row, put the rest of the Age A civil deck on the current age board. The military side of the current age board stays empty for now. Players will never draw Age A military cards. Keep the Age I and Age II decks nearby for later use.

walkthrough.title: Determine Starting Player and First-Round Actions
walkthrough.text: Randomly choose a starting player. Remember who this is, as play order matters throughout the game. For the first round, players have limited actions. The starting player puts 1 white token on their Despotism card, giving them 1 civil action. The second player puts 2 white tokens on their card. The third player puts 3, and the fourth puts 4. No military actions are available in round one. All remaining action tokens stay beside the board. You are ready to play.

bullets: Each player takes a player board in their chosen color
Leave plenty of space around your board for your civilization to grow

bullets: 7 wooden pieces per player: 2 octagonal counters, 4 round markers, 1 tactics standard
Counters track culture and science points; markers track ratings

summary.title: Set Up Your Blue Bank
bullets: Place 16 blue tokens on your blue bank squares
Blue tokens = food and resources; empty bank sections = corruption

Remaining Age A civil deck goes on current age board

summary.title: Determine Starting Player and First-Round Actions

No military actions in round one; only card-taking actions available

walkthrough.title: Game Overview and Ages
walkthrough.text: Through the Ages spans four ages: Age A (Antiquity), Age I (Medieval), Age II (Age of Exploration), and Age III (Modern Era). Each age brings more powerful technologies, leaders, and wonders. The game flows through rounds where each player takes one full turn. A turn consists of replenishing the card row, an optional Politics Phase, an Action Phase, and an End-of-Turn Sequence including production. The civilization with the most culture points at the end wins.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure
walkthrough.text: Each turn follows this order. First, replenish the card row: remove the leftmost cards based on player count (1 card in 4-player, 2 in 3-player, 3 in 2-player), slide remaining cards left, and deal new civil cards from the current deck to fill empty spaces on the right. Second, make your exclusive tactic available by moving it to the common tactics area. Third, the Politics Phase where you may play at most one political action. Fourth, the Action Phase where you spend civil and military actions. Fifth, the End-of-Turn Sequence covering production and card draws.

walkthrough.title: Civil and Military Actions
walkthrough.text: Your government card determines how many civil actions (white tokens) and military actions (red tokens) you have each turn. Despotism gives 4 civil and 2 military. To perform an action, move a token off your government card. You can perform actions in any order and can alternate between civil and military actions. You do not have to use all your actions. At the end of your turn, all tokens reset back onto your government card.

walkthrough.title: Taking Cards from the Card Row
walkthrough.text: Taking a card costs civil actions based on its position in the card row: 1 for the first five positions, 2 for the next four, 3 for the last four. Leaders go into your hand. You may never take two leaders from the same age. Wonders go directly into play sideways as unfinished wonders. You can only have one unfinished wonder at a time. Technology cards and action cards go into your hand. You cannot take a technology with the same name as one already in your hand or in play. Your civil card hand limit equals your number of white tokens.

walkthrough.title: Increasing Population
walkthrough.text: To grow your civilization, you can increase population for 1 civil action. Take the rightmost yellow token from your yellow bank and place it in your worker pool as an unused worker. You must pay a food cost shown under that section of the yellow bank. Early increases cost 2 food, but as your population grows and sections empty, the cost rises. Emptying sections also reveals consumption requirements and increases your need for happy faces to prevent discontent workers.

walkthrough.title: Building Farms, Mines, and Urban Buildings
walkthrough.text: To build a farm or mine, pay 1 civil action plus the resource cost shown on the technology card, then move an unused worker onto that card. For urban buildings like labs, temples, libraries, theaters, and arenas, it works the same way: 1 civil action plus the resource cost, then move an unused worker onto the card. Urban buildings have a limit per type set by your government card. Despotism limits you to 2 buildings of each type. Building a military unit costs 1 military action instead of a civil action, plus resources.

walkthrough.title: Upgrading and Destroying
walkthrough.text: If you have a higher-level technology of the same type as an existing building or unit, you can upgrade. Pay 1 civil action (or 1 military action for units), pay resources equal to the difference between the new and old resource costs, and move the worker from the lower card to the higher one. Update your ratings. You can also destroy a farm, mine, or urban building for 1 civil action, or disband a military unit for 1 military action, returning the worker to your worker pool. You get no resources back.

walkthrough.title: Developing Technologies
walkthrough.text: To put a technology card from your hand into play, pay 1 civil action and pay the science cost shown in the upper left corner of the card. Move your science points counter backward on the track. Farm and mine technologies stack above existing ones of the same type. Urban building and military unit technologies are organized by type icon. Special technologies (blue) take effect immediately and never need workers. If you develop a new special technology of the same type, the old one is automatically removed.

walkthrough.title: Playing Leaders
walkthrough.text: To play a leader from your hand, pay 1 civil action and place them next to your player board. Their effect immediately applies. If you already have a leader in play, the old leader is automatically removed and you gain 1 civil action back. A leader's effects may include symbols at the bottom that change your ratings, plus text describing special abilities. Leaders like Caesar give extra military actions and strength. Moses adjusts population increase rules. You may take a leader and play it on the same turn.

walkthrough.title: Building Wonders
walkthrough.text: Wonders enter play unfinished and sideways when taken from the card row. Each wonder has stages with resource costs shown at the bottom. To build one stage, pay 1 civil action and the resource cost, then cover the stage number with a blue token from your blue bank. You can build multiple stages per turn if you have enough actions and resources. When the last stage is built, the wonder is completed: return all blue tokens to your blue bank, straighten the card, and apply its effects. Taking a new wonder costs 1 extra civil action per already-completed wonder.

walkthrough.title: Changing Governments
walkthrough.text: There are two ways to change your government. Peaceful change costs 1 civil action plus the higher science cost on the card. The new government replaces the old one, and you update your action totals and ratings. Revolution costs all your civil actions (they must all be unspent) plus the lower science cost. Any new civil actions gained from the revolution are immediately spent without effect. Your military actions are not affected by revolution and can still be used before or after.

walkthrough.title: Playing Action Cards
walkthrough.text: Action cards provide one-time benefits and are discarded after use. An action card cannot be played the same turn it was taken from the card row. To play one, pay 1 civil action and follow the card text. Effects include scoring science or culture points, gaining food or resources, performing actions at a discount, or gaining temporary military resources. Some action cards like Breakthrough can even be used to declare a revolution.

walkthrough.title: Politics Phase: Events and Territories
walkthrough.text: Before your Action Phase, you may play one political action. In the basic game, this means preparing an event. Place a green military card with the event symbol face down on the future events space, score culture equal to the card's level, then reveal and evaluate the top card of the current events deck. When the current events deck runs out, the future events deck is shuffled and becomes the new current events deck. Events can reward or punish based on strength comparisons. In the full game, you can also play aggressions, wars, and pacts.

walkthrough.title: Colonization
walkthrough.text: When a territory is revealed as a current event, all players may bid to colonize it. Bidding starts with the active player and proceeds clockwise. The highest bidder must send a colonization force of military units equal to or greater than their bid. The colonization force is calculated as: unit strengths plus army tactical strength plus colonization modifiers from cards in play, plus military bonus cards played. Units sent for colonization are sacrificed and their yellow tokens return to the yellow bank. The colony card provides permanent effects and an immediate one-time benefit.

walkthrough.title: Tactics and Armies
walkthrough.text: Playing a tactics card costs 1 military action. Place it in your play area with your tactics standard on it as your current tactic. It stays exclusive for one round, then moves to the common tactics area where anyone can copy it for 2 military actions. Your current tactic lets military units form armies: each set of unit types shown on the card makes one army with additional tactical strength added to your strength rating. Age II and III tactics have two strength values: the higher for normal armies and the lower for outdated armies containing units two or more levels below the tactic.

walkthrough.title: Happiness and Discontent Workers
walkthrough.text: As your yellow bank empties, subsections reveal numbers indicating how many happy faces you need. Happy faces come from temples, certain wonders, and leaders. Your number of discontent workers equals the empty subsections to the left of your happiness marker. Discontent workers are covered by unused workers. If discontent workers outnumber unused workers, you face an uprising and skip your entire Production Phase. You can fix this by building happiness-producing buildings, increasing population for more unused workers, or upgrading temples.

walkthrough.title: End-of-Turn Sequence: Corruption and Production
walkthrough.text: After your Action Phase, follow the End-of-Turn Sequence printed on your player board. First, discard excess military cards down to your red token count. Then enter your Production Phase. Check for uprising first. If no uprising, score science and culture points equal to your ratings. Then check corruption: if negative numbers in your blue bank are uncovered, you lose that many resources, paying food if resources run short. Then produce food by placing 1 blue token per farm worker. Then check consumption: uncovered negative numbers in the yellow bank show food consumed, with a 4 culture point penalty per unpaid food. Then produce resources by placing 1 blue token per mine worker.

walkthrough.title: Drawing Military Cards and Resetting Actions
walkthrough.text: After production, draw 1 military card from the current military deck for each unused military action, up to a maximum of 3 cards. Military cards are kept secret. Then reset all your actions by returning all white and red tokens to your government card. Your turn is complete and the next player begins.

walkthrough.title: End of an Age
walkthrough.text: When a civil deck runs out while replenishing the card row, the current age ends. Players discard all cards from the previous age in their hands. Leaders and unfinished wonders from the previous age are removed from play. Each player loses 2 yellow tokens from their yellow bank. Then the new age's civil and military decks are shuffled and placed on the current age board. Completed wonders and developed technologies remain in play regardless of age.

walkthrough.title: Aggressions, Wars, and Pacts (Full Game)
walkthrough.text: In the full game, you can play aggressions and wars as political actions. An aggression targets one player: the attacker spends military actions and the defender can play defense bonus cards or discard any military card for +1 bonus each, up to their military action total. If the defender cannot match the attacker's strength, the aggression succeeds. Wars are declared but resolved one round later based on cards in play only. Pacts are agreements between two players providing mutual benefits but can be cancelled or become antiquated. In 2-player games, pacts are removed.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends when the last civil deck runs out and all players have had equal turns. In the full game, final scoring comes from Age III events: all remaining Age III events in the current and future events decks are evaluated, awarding culture points based on various civilization achievements. In the first game, final scoring instead awards points for: 1 culture per Age I technology and 2 per Age II technology (government counts double), plus culture equal to your science, strength, and happiness ratings, plus food and resource production, plus 3 per colony and 6 per completed Age II wonder. Highest culture points wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome and Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round and into the second round with three example players. Player 1 is the starting player, Player 2 is second, and Player 3 is third. We will see different strategies and watch Player 3 make some classic beginner mistakes. Follow along even if your player count differs. Ready? Let's build some civilizations.

walkthrough.title: Round 1: Limited Actions
walkthrough.text: In the first round, players only take cards from the card row. Player 1 has 1 civil action, Player 2 has 2, and Player 3 has 3. There are no military actions available yet. The card row has 13 Age A cards including leaders, wonders, and action cards. Remember, the first five cards cost 1 action, the next four cost 2, and the last four cost 3.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Take a Leader
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has just 1 civil action. They scan the card row and spot Moses in the first section. Moses does not change any statistics, but his special text adjusts the rules for increasing population, making it cheaper. Player 1 takes Moses into their hand. That is their only action. Now they perform the End-of-Turn Sequence: score 1 science point for their lab, produce 2 food on Agriculture, and produce 2 resources on Bronze. Simple first turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Take a Wonder and Action Card
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has 2 civil actions. They spend 1 action to take the Colossus wonder from the first section and place it sideways next to their board as an unfinished wonder. Then they spend their second action to take the Engineering Genius action card, which will help them build the Colossus more cheaply on a future turn. End-of-Turn: score 1 science, produce 2 food and 2 resources.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn: The Big Spender
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has 3 civil actions and wants to use them all. They spend 2 actions to take Hammurabi from the middle section of the card row. That is an Age A leader. Then they spend their last action on a cheap action card from the first section. End-of-Turn is the same: 1 science, 2 food, 2 resources. Round 1 is done.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 Begins: All Actions Available
walkthrough.text: From round 2 onward, everyone has all their actions: 4 civil and 2 military. Each player starts their turn by replenishing the card row. When the starting player replenishes for the first time, Age A ends and Age I begins. The rest of the Age A civil deck is removed, the Age I civil deck is shuffled and placed on the current age board, and the Age I military deck becomes available for drawing.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Play Leader and Build
walkthrough.text: Player 1 replenishes the card row, triggering the age transition. Now they have 4 civil actions. First, they play Moses from their hand for 1 civil action. Moses enters play and his population discount applies. Next, they spend 1 civil action and 2 food to increase population, getting a new unused worker. Then they spend 1 civil action and 2 resources to build a new mine on Bronze. Their last civil action goes to taking an Age I technology from the card row. They leave both military actions unused, so they draw 2 military cards at the end of their turn.

walkthrough.title: Why Player 1's Turn Was Strong
walkthrough.text: Player 1 made every action count. Playing Moses immediately gave them a discount on population increases. Growing population gave them a worker to build a mine, which will increase resource production going forward. Taking a technology sets up a future development. And by saving military actions, they drew 2 military cards for future political actions and tactics. This is the kind of efficient, forward-thinking play that wins games.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Build the Wonder
walkthrough.text: Player 2 replenishes the card row. Then they play the Engineering Genius action card for 1 civil action, building one stage of the Colossus at a discount. They spend another civil action to build the second stage at normal cost. The Colossus is now complete. They return the blue tokens to their blue bank and straighten the card. The Colossus gives them a strength bonus. Smart use of the action card to save resources.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Ignoring Military
walkthrough.text: Player 3 replenishes the card row. They play Hammurabi from their hand for 1 civil action. Then they spend 1 civil action to increase population, 1 to build a farm, and take their last civil action to grab another technology card. But here is the mistake: Player 3 completely ignores their 2 military actions. They do not build any military units and leave both military actions unused. They draw 2 military cards, sure, but they have no plan for strengthening their civilization defensively.

walkthrough.title: Why Ignoring Military Is Dangerous
walkthrough.text: Player 3's strength rating is still only 1 from their starting warrior. Meanwhile, Player 2 just completed the Colossus for a strength bonus. As the game progresses, events will start rewarding the strongest players and punishing the weakest. Even in a peaceful first game with no direct attacks, falling behind in strength means losing out on event bonuses. In the full game, Player 3 would be a sitting duck for aggressions and wars. Never completely ignore your military.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Second Mistake: Not Managing Corruption
walkthrough.text: A few rounds later, Player 3 has been building farms and mines, producing food and resources every turn. But they have been stockpiling without spending efficiently. Their blue bank is getting dangerously empty as blue tokens pile up on their technology cards. During their Production Phase, the corruption step hits: with 5 empty blue bank squares, they face corruption of 2, losing 2 resources they needed for building. Player 3 is shocked. They did not realize that hoarding food and resources would backfire.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Spend Before You Produce
walkthrough.text: Corruption punishes players who let food and resources accumulate without spending them. The key is to spend your resources during your Action Phase before production adds more tokens. Build structures, upgrade technologies, or complete wonder stages to keep your blue bank from emptying. Also, upgrading to higher-level farm and mine technologies helps because each blue token on a higher card represents more value, so you need fewer tokens overall to store the same amount.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Third Mistake: Forgetting Happiness
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has been growing their population aggressively. Their yellow bank has emptied several subsections, revealing that they need 3 happy faces to keep everyone happy. But Player 3's happiness rating is only 1, from a single temple they built. That means they have 2 discontent workers. They only have 1 unused worker to cover a discontent slot. Discontent workers outnumber unused workers, so Player 3 faces an uprising and skips their entire Production Phase. No science, no culture, no food, no resources.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Balance Population Growth with Happiness
walkthrough.text: Every time you increase population, check the happiness indicator. If you are about to empty a subsection that pushes your required happy faces above your happiness rating, you need to build temples, arenas, or complete wonders that provide happy faces first. An uprising is devastating: losing an entire Production Phase means no points scored and no resources generated. Always keep happiness in sync with population growth.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Recovery
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has learned their lessons. On their next turn, they spend 1 civil action and resources to build a temple, increasing both happiness and culture rating. They use a military action to build a warrior, boosting their strength. They carefully spend resources before production to avoid corruption. No uprising, production runs smoothly, and they even prepare an event to score a culture point. Player 3 is back on track.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues with each player taking turns. The card row keeps refreshing with new Age I cards, then eventually Age II cards. Leaders come and go, wonders get built, technologies get developed, and the political landscape shifts as events reward the prepared and punish the careless. Keep building your economy, maintain your military, and always watch your happiness and corruption.

walkthrough.title: End of the Game
walkthrough.text: When the final civil deck runs out and all players have had equal turns, the game ends. In the full game, all remaining Age III events are evaluated for final scoring. Players who prepared favorable events earlier reap the rewards. The civilization with the most culture points wins. Think about what Age III events you want to seed into the future events deck throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen the core actions in play, watched Player 3 learn from three critical mistakes: ignoring military strength, failing to manage corruption, and forgetting about happiness. These are the three traps that catch nearly every new Through the Ages player. Keep them in mind and you will avoid the worst pitfalls. You are ready to build a civilization that will stand the test of time.

walkthrough.title: Before You Start Your Civilization
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics like when to increase population and how to balance your economy.

Second, reference the Appendix for quick definitions of card types, leaders, wonders, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun building your story!

summary.title: Welcome and Meet the Players
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 players demonstrating different strategies
Player 3 will make classic beginner mistakes to learn from
Covers first round through early second round

summary.title: Round 1: Limited Actions
bullets: Players start with 1/2/3 civil actions only (no military)
13 Age A cards in the card row: leaders, wonders, action cards
Cost: 1 action (first 5), 2 actions (next 4), 3 actions (last 4)

summary.title: Player 1's Turn: Take a Leader
bullets: Player 1 takes Moses (population discount) for 1 civil action
End-of-Turn: score 1 science, produce 2 food + 2 resources

summary.title: Player 2's Turn: Take a Wonder and Action Card
bullets: Takes Colossus wonder (placed sideways as unfinished) for 1 action
Takes Engineering Genius action card for 1 action
Plans to use the action card to build the wonder cheaply later

summary.title: Player 3's Turn: The Big Spender
bullets: Spends 2 actions to take Hammurabi from the middle section
Uses last action on a cheap action card
All 3 actions used, end-of-turn as normal

summary.title: Round 2 Begins: All Actions Available
bullets: 4 civil + 2 military actions available for all players
Card row replenished; Age A ends, Age I begins
Age I civil and military decks become active

summary.title: Player 1's Turn: Play Leader and Build
bullets: Plays Moses, increases population, builds a mine, takes a tech
Leaves military actions unused to draw 2 military cards
Every action planned for maximum efficiency

summary.title: Why Player 1's Turn Was Strong
bullets: Leader played immediately for ongoing benefit
Population growth enabled building; tech taken for future development
Saved military actions = drew military cards for future use

summary.title: Player 2's Turn: Build the Wonder
bullets: Uses Engineering Genius for discounted wonder stage
Completes the Colossus in one turn for strength bonus
Action cards save resources when used at the right moment

summary.title: Player 3's Mistake: Ignoring Military
bullets: Plays leader, grows population, builds farm, takes tech
Completely ignores military actions and strength building
Strength rating stuck at 1 while others grow stronger

summary.title: Why Ignoring Military Is Dangerous
bullets: Events punish weakest and reward strongest civilizations
Full game: low strength invites aggressions and wars
Even in peaceful variants, military strength matters for events

summary.title: Player 3's Second Mistake: Not Managing Corruption
bullets: Stockpiled food and resources without spending efficiently
Blue bank emptied, triggering corruption of 2
Lost resources they needed for building

summary.title: Lesson: Spend Before You Produce
bullets: Spend resources during Action Phase before production adds more
Upgrade to higher-level techs to store more with fewer tokens
Keep blue bank from emptying to avoid corruption

summary.title: Player 3's Third Mistake: Forgetting Happiness
bullets: Grew population aggressively; yellow bank emptied subsections
Needed 3 happy faces, had only 1 (from one temple)
Discontent workers > unused workers = uprising, skip Production Phase

summary.title: Lesson: Balance Population Growth with Happiness
bullets: Check happiness indicator before increasing population
Build temples, arenas, or wonders for happy faces first
Uprising = no science, culture, food, or resource production

summary.title: Player 3's Recovery
bullets: Builds temple (happiness + culture), builds warrior (strength)
Spends resources before production to avoid corruption
Prepares an event to score culture; back on track

summary.title: Continue Playing
bullets: Card row refreshes with Age I, then Age II cards
Leaders evolve, wonders get built, politics shift
Balance economy, military, and happiness throughout

summary.title: End of the Game
bullets: Game ends when last civil deck runs out and turns are equalized
Full game: Age III events determine final scoring
Plan which events to seed into future events deck throughout

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Practiced core actions and watched Player 3 learn from 3 mistakes
Never ignore military, manage corruption, balance happiness
You are ready to build a civilization that stands the test of time

summary.title: Before You Start Your Civilization
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics
Reference the Appendix for card types, leaders, and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime via Q&A tab

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make. First, ignoring military strength, which leads to losing events, being vulnerable to aggressions, and getting punished by wars. Second, growing population too fast without building happiness, which causes uprisings. Third, hoarding food and resources until corruption eats them away. Fourth, taking cards from the card row that you cannot afford to develop or build for many turns, wasting hand space. Fifth, neglecting culture production, since culture points are the only thing that matters for winning.

walkthrough.title: Balance Your Economy Early
walkthrough.text: In the early game, focus on building a balanced economy. You need food to grow population, resources to build things, and science to develop technologies. A common opening is to increase population once, build a mine or farm, and take one or two technology cards. Do not overcommit to any one resource. If you build three mines but no farms, you will have plenty of resources but no food to grow your workforce. If you ignore resources, you cannot build anything. Keep food and resources roughly in balance.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Blue Bank Carefully
walkthrough.text: Your blue bank has 16 tokens. Every time you produce food and resources, tokens leave the bank. Corruption happens when sections of your bank are empty during your Production Phase. The key insight is that corruption is checked before production. So plan your spending during the Action Phase to return tokens to the bank before production fills cards up again. Upgrading to higher-level farms and mines is one of the best ways to manage corruption, since each token on a higher card represents more value while using fewer physical tokens.

walkthrough.title: Develop Technologies Aggressively
walkthrough.text: Technologies are the backbone of your civilization. Higher-level farms and mines produce more per worker. Better urban buildings provide more science, culture, and happiness. Advanced military units are stronger. And better governments give you more actions. Accumulate science points and spend them on technologies that match your strategy. Do not sit on 15 science points for half the game. That is wasted potential. Develop technologies as soon as you can afford them, especially farm and mine upgrades in the early to mid game.

walkthrough.title: Population Growth Is Essential But Dangerous
walkthrough.text: Workers are the engine of your civilization. Every farm, mine, building, and military unit requires a worker. You start with 7 workers and can grow to a maximum of 25. But each new worker comes with costs: food to pay for the increase, higher consumption in your yellow bank, and greater happiness requirements. The golden rule is to grow population only when you have something productive for the new worker to do and enough happiness to support them. Never grow just because you can.

walkthrough.title: Keep Military Strength Competitive
walkthrough.text: You do not need to have the strongest military, but you should avoid being the weakest. Events frequently reward the strongest player and punish the weakest. In the full game, aggressions and wars can devastate a weak civilization. Aim to stay in the middle of the pack at minimum. Building one extra military unit, developing a tactic, and keeping a few military bonus cards for defense is usually enough to stay safe without over-investing in military.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Leaders and Wonders
walkthrough.text: Leaders and wonders are the most powerful cards in the game, but they require careful planning. Take a leader only if you can play them soon, since they sit in your hand doing nothing until played. For wonders, make sure you can build all the stages before the wonder becomes antiquated at an age transition. Unfinished wonders block you from taking new ones and their blue tokens sitting on stages contribute to corruption risk. The best wonders are those that align with your overall strategy.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Card Row Timing
walkthrough.text: The card row is a conveyor belt. Cards slide left and become cheaper over time, but they also get removed from the leftmost positions at the start of each turn. A card you want might disappear before your next turn. If a critical technology or leader is in the danger zone, consider grabbing it now even if it is expensive. Conversely, if a card you want is far right, you might wait a turn for it to slide left and become cheaper. Reading the card row timing is a skill that develops with experience.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Events Strategically
walkthrough.text: Preparing events is one of the most important political actions. You score culture points when you prepare an event, and you choose which events enter the future events deck. Try to prepare events that will benefit your civilization when they are revealed later. If you are strong militarily, seed events that reward the strongest. If you have high science, seed events that reward science. You also get to reveal and evaluate a current event when you prepare, so timing your political action can be crucial.

walkthrough.title: Culture Is Everything
walkthrough.text: Never forget that the only thing that matters at the end of the game is culture points. Science, strength, resources, and population are all means to an end. In the mid to late game, shift your focus toward culture production. Temples, theaters, and arenas produce culture. Wonders provide culture bonuses. Preparing events scores culture. In the full game, Age III events are the biggest source of final scoring, so pay attention to what types of achievements they reward and build your civilization accordingly.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't ignore military — events punish the weakest
Don't grow population without happiness — uprisings skip Production Phase
Don't hoard resources — corruption eats them
Don't take cards you can't afford to play soon
Don't neglect culture — it is the only victory condition

summary.title: Balance Your Economy Early
bullets: Food for population, resources for building, science for tech
Common opening: increase population, build mine/farm, take tech cards
Keep food and resources roughly balanced; don't overspecialize

summary.title: Manage Your Blue Bank Carefully
bullets: 16 blue tokens total; empty sections = corruption
Corruption checked before production — spend during Action Phase
Higher-level techs store more value per token, reducing corruption risk

summary.title: Develop Technologies Aggressively
bullets: Higher techs = more production, better buildings, stronger military
Don't hoard science points — develop techs as soon as affordable
Farm and mine upgrades are especially important early-to-mid game

summary.title: Population Growth Is Essential But Dangerous
bullets: Workers drive everything — farms, mines, buildings, military all need them
Each new worker = higher food cost, consumption, happiness needs
Grow only when you have a job for the new worker and happiness to support them

summary.title: Keep Military Strength Competitive
bullets: Don't need to be strongest, but avoid being weakest
Events punish weakest; full game adds aggressions and wars
One extra unit + a tactic + bonus cards for defense usually suffices

summary.title: Plan Your Leaders and Wonders
bullets: Take leaders only if you can play them soon (hand space is limited)
Build wonders before they become antiquated at age transitions
Unfinished wonders block new wonder takes and risk corruption

summary.title: Watch the Card Row Timing
bullets: Cards slide left each turn, getting cheaper but approaching removal
Grab critical cards before opponents or removal; wait for non-urgent cards to slide
Card row reading improves with experience

summary.title: Prepare Events Strategically
bullets: Preparing events scores culture and seeds favorable future events
Match events to your strengths — military events if strong, science events if advanced
Timing your political action can be crucial

summary.title: Culture Is Everything
bullets: Culture points are the ONLY victory condition
Mid-to-late game: shift toward culture production (temples, theaters, arenas)
Age III events are biggest source of final scoring — build for what they reward

walkthrough.title: The Science Rush
walkthrough.text: One of the most powerful openings is to prioritize science production above all else. Build a second lab early and develop Philosophy or Alchemy as soon as you can afford them. High science lets you develop technologies faster than opponents, gaining access to better farms, mines, and buildings before anyone else. The compound effect is enormous: better farms produce more food, which lets you grow population, which lets you build more, which produces more science. Leaders like Aristotle boost this strategy with science bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Military Aggression Strategy
walkthrough.text: In the full game, a strong military is not just defensive. It is a win condition. Build multiple military units early, develop infantry and cavalry technologies, and play tactics cards for army bonuses. Leaders like Alexander the Great and Napoleon amplify military strength. The strategy works by preparing events that reward the strongest, playing aggressions to steal resources from weaker players, and declaring wars to destroy opponents' economies. The culture cost of maintaining a large military is offset by the culture you gain from winning conflicts and favorable events.

walkthrough.title: Wonder-Centric Play
walkthrough.text: Wonders provide some of the most powerful effects in the game. A wonder-focused strategy prioritizes resource production and construction technologies to build wonders quickly. Construction special technologies reduce urban building costs and let you build multiple wonder stages for a single civil action. Leaders like Hammurabi and later Leonardo da Vinci can facilitate wonder construction. The key is to complete wonders before the age ends, since unfinished wonders are removed. Completed wonders stay forever and stack their benefits.

walkthrough.title: Government Timing
walkthrough.text: Your government determines how many actions you get per turn, and more actions means more things accomplished. A revolution is cheaper in science but costs all your civil actions for a turn. A peaceful change is more expensive in science but lets you keep acting. The optimal timing depends on your situation. If you have excess science and need actions immediately, go peaceful. If you can afford to lose a turn of civil actions, revolution early for a government like Monarchy or Republic. The best players plan their government transitions two or three turns ahead.

walkthrough.title: Event Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Advanced players use the event system as a strategic weapon. By choosing which events to prepare, you can shape the future events deck to favor your civilization. If you are building toward military dominance, seed the deck with events that reward the strongest. If you have high culture production, seed events that punish low culture. The timing matters too: events placed in the future deck may not be revealed for several turns, so plan ahead. Scoring culture for preparing events is itself a reliable source of points, especially with Age II and III events worth 2 and 3 culture each.

walkthrough.title: The Population Engine
walkthrough.text: An aggressive population strategy builds the largest workforce possible. More workers means more farms, mines, buildings, and military units. Leaders like Moses reduce population costs, and colonies can provide free population. The danger is happiness management: you need temples, arenas, or happiness-producing wonders to prevent uprisings. The payoff is enormous though. A civilization with 15 workers producing resources and culture across advanced technologies will outproduce one with 10 workers, even if the smaller civilization has slightly better technology.

walkthrough.title: Age III Event Positioning
walkthrough.text: In the full game, Age III events determine the majority of final scoring. Each event rewards specific achievements: some score based on the number of urban buildings, others based on military strength, science rating, or completed wonders. Experienced players begin positioning for Age III events in Age II. If you know Impact of Science is in the event deck, push your science rating as high as possible. If you know Impact of Strength will be scored, maintain a strong military through the endgame. Seeding your own Age III events into the future events deck lets you control what gets scored.

walkthrough.title: Colonization Timing
walkthrough.text: Colonies provide powerful permanent bonuses and immediate benefits, but colonizing requires sacrificing military units. The best time to colonize is when you have redundant military units or can quickly rebuild. Colonization modifiers from special technologies like Cartography make your force more efficient. Military bonus cards augment your colonization force without spending units. The worst time to colonize is right before you expect to face an aggression, since your strength drops after sacrificing units. Time your colonization when other players are focused elsewhere.

walkthrough.title: Corruption Optimization
walkthrough.text: Advanced players treat corruption not as a problem to avoid but as a resource to manage. Sometimes it is worth having mild corruption if it means your farms and mines are fully loaded with high-value blue tokens. The key calculation is whether the resources you lose to corruption are worth less than the benefit of having those resources available during your Action Phase. Construction technologies that reduce building costs and higher-level techs that store more value per token are the tools for mastering corruption management.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Culture Sprint
walkthrough.text: The last two or three turns of the game are where culture production matters most. Every culture point scored in your Production Phase during these turns directly impacts your final score. By this point, you should have theaters and arenas producing culture, completed wonders adding bonuses, and a strong government giving you enough actions to prepare high-value events. The players who win are those who transition smoothly from building their economy in Ages I and II to maximizing culture output in Age III. A civilization that produces 10 culture per turn for the last three turns gains 30 points, often the difference between winning and losing.

summary.title: The Science Rush
bullets: Prioritize labs and science tech early for compound tech advantage
Better techs produce more, which produces more science, which snowballs
Aristotle and Alchemy amplify science production
Lets you develop technologies before opponents can compete

summary.title: Military Aggression Strategy
bullets: Build units, develop tactics, use leaders like Alexander and Napoleon
Prepare events that reward strongest, play aggressions to steal
Culture cost of military offset by conflict winnings and event bonuses
Declare wars when opponents are weak, especially after they colonize

summary.title: Wonder-Centric Play
bullets: Prioritize resources and construction techs for fast wonder completion
Construction technologies let you build multiple stages for 1 action
Complete wonders before age transitions or lose them
Hammurabi and Leonardo da Vinci facilitate wonder strategies

summary.title: Government Timing
bullets: Revolution: cheaper in science but costs all civil actions for a turn
Peaceful change: more science but keeps you active
Plan government transitions 2-3 turns ahead
More actions per turn = exponential advantage over the game

summary.title: Event Manipulation
bullets: Seed future events deck with events matching your strengths
Age II/III events score 2-3 culture just for preparing them
Control what events get scored by preparing them strategically
Events placed now may not reveal for several turns — plan ahead

summary.title: The Population Engine
bullets: More workers = more farms, mines, buildings, and military units
Moses reduces population costs; colonies give free population
Must pair with happiness production to prevent uprisings
15 workers on advanced techs outproduce 10 workers on better techs

summary.title: Age III Event Positioning
bullets: Age III events determine majority of full-game final scoring
Begin positioning in Age II for expected scoring categories
Impact of Science, Impact of Strength, etc. — build toward them
Seeding your own Age III events lets you control final scoring

summary.title: Colonization Timing
bullets: Colonies provide permanent bonuses but cost military units
Best time: when you have redundant units or can rebuild quickly
Cartography and bonus cards make colonization more efficient
Worst time: right before expected aggression (strength drops)

summary.title: Corruption Optimization
bullets: Mild corruption acceptable if resources are actively useful
Higher-level techs store more value per token, reducing corruption
Construction techs reduce building costs, leaving fewer idle resources
Calculate whether corruption cost < benefit of available resources

summary.title: Endgame Culture Sprint
bullets: Last 2-3 turns: culture production directly determines winner
Theaters, arenas, completed wonders, and event preparation all contribute
10 culture/turn for 3 turns = 30 points, often the winning margin
Transition from economy-building (Ages I-II) to culture-maximizing (Age III)

entries.definition: Represented by white tokens. Used for taking cards, increasing population, building farms/mines/urban buildings, developing technologies, playing leaders, building wonder stages, playing action cards, and changing governments. Your total is determined by your government card and other modifiers.

Represented by red tokens. Used for building and upgrading military units, disbanding units, playing tactics cards, and copying tactics. Unspent military actions let you draw military cards at end of turn (max 3). Your total is determined by your government card and other modifiers.

The victory condition. Scored during production based on your culture rating, and also from preparing events, action cards, and other effects. Tracked on the culture points track with your octagonal counter. The player with the most culture points at game end wins.

How many culture points your civilization produces each turn during the Production Phase. Increased by building temples, theaters, arenas, completing certain wonders, and playing certain leaders. Tracked on the culture rating indicator.

Accumulated during production based on your science rating. Spent to develop technologies and change governments. Tracked on the science points track with your octagonal counter. Cannot go below zero.

How many science points your civilization produces each turn. Increased by building labs and libraries, and by certain wonders and leaders. Tracked on the science rating indicator. You start at 1 from your initial lab.

The might of your civilization's military. Calculated from military unit strengths plus tactical army bonuses plus modifiers from cards. Relevant for events, aggressions, wars, and colonization. Tracked on the strength rating indicator. You start at 1 from your initial warrior.

Number of happy faces your civilization produces, from temples, arenas, certain wonders, and leaders. Maximum of 8. When your yellow bank empties subsections, you need enough happiness to prevent discontent workers. Tracked on the happiness indicator above the yellow bank.

Holds 16 blue tokens representing stored food and resources. Tokens move from the bank onto farm and mine technology cards during production. Empty sections reveal negative numbers representing corruption. Blue bank is refilled from left to right when tokens are returned.

Holds yellow tokens representing available land. Tokens move from the bank to the worker pool when you increase population. Emptying sections reveals consumption requirements (food your people eat each turn) and increases the need for happy faces. The bank has 18 tokens at game start and shrinks by 2 at each age transition.

Where unused workers reside. Workers in the pool are available to be placed on technology cards via build actions. You start with 1 unused worker. Discontent workers may be placed on happiness indicator spaces but still count as unused.

Occurs when sections of your blue bank are empty during the corruption step of your Production Phase. You lose resources equal to the leftmost uncovered negative number. If resources are insufficient, you pay the difference with food. Managed by spending efficiently and upgrading technologies.

Food your population eats each turn during the consumption step of production. Equal to the leftmost uncovered negative number in your yellow bank. If you cannot pay enough food, you lose 4 culture points per unpaid food. Increases as your population grows.

Occurs when discontent workers outnumber unused workers. You skip your entire Production Phase: no science, no culture, no food production, no resource production. Prevented by increasing happiness rating or having enough unused workers to cover discontent.

Workers who are unhappy due to insufficient happy faces. The number of discontent workers equals the number of empty yellow bank subsections to the left of your happiness marker. If discontent workers exceed unused workers, an uprising occurs.

The 13-space display of civil cards that players draft from. Cards cost 1 civil action (positions 1-5), 2 (positions 6-9), or 3 (positions 10-13). At the start of each turn (after Round 1), the leftmost cards are removed, remaining cards slide left, and new cards fill from the right.
Holds the current age's civil deck (for dealing to card row) and military deck (for player draws). When an age ends, new decks are placed here. In the final age, the board is empty.

The face-down deck of events on the military board. When a player prepares a future event, the top current event is revealed and evaluated. Initially filled with Age A events from setup. When exhausted, the future events deck becomes the new current events deck.

Events placed here by players during their Politics Phase. When the current events deck is exhausted, the future events deck is shuffled and becomes the new current events deck. Players choose which events to seed, adding a strategic layer to event manipulation.

Space on the military board where tactics cards become publicly available. Any player can copy a tactic from this area for 2 military actions. Players' tactics standards mark their current tactic. Multiple standards can be on the same card.
A player-colored wooden piece placed on a tactics card to mark your current tactic. Starts in the common tactics area with no current tactic. Moved to a card when you play or copy a tactic.

Technology cards that allow building farms. Workers on farm cards produce food during production (1 blue token per worker). Higher-level farms produce more food per token. Agriculture is the starting farm technology. Upgradeable to Irrigation, Selective Breeding, and Mechanized Agriculture.

Technology cards that allow building mines. Workers on mine cards produce resources during production (1 blue token per worker). Higher-level mines produce more resources per token. Bronze is the starting mine technology. Upgradeable to Iron, Coal, and Oil.

Technology cards for labs, temples, libraries, theaters, and arenas. Workers on these cards produce science, culture, happiness, or strength depending on type. Limited per type by your government's urban building limit. Upgraded within the same type.

Technology cards for infantry, cavalry, artillery, and air forces (Age III only). Workers on these cards are military units contributing to strength rating. Built and upgraded with military actions instead of civil actions. No building limit applies.

Technology cards that take effect immediately when developed, without needing workers. Types include construction, colonization, warfare, and others. You cannot have two of the same type in play; the lower-level one is automatically replaced.

Your government card determines civil actions, military actions, and urban building limit. You start with Despotism (4 civil, 2 military, limit 2). Changed via peaceful change (1 civil action + higher science) or revolution (all civil actions + lower science). Examples include Monarchy, Theocracy, Republic, Constitutional Monarchy, Democracy, and Communism.

Great historical figures providing special abilities. Only 1 leader in play at a time. Max 1 leader taken per age. Playing costs 1 civil action; replacing an existing leader returns 1 civil action. Leaders include historical figures like Moses, Caesar, Aristotle, Hammurabi, Homer, and many more across all ages.

Epic constructions providing powerful permanent effects once completed. Enter play unfinished (sideways) when taken from card row. Built in stages, each costing 1 civil action + resources. Only 1 unfinished wonder allowed. Taking a new wonder costs +1 civil action per completed wonder.

One-time use cards providing immediate benefits like resources, science, culture, or discounted actions. Cannot be played the same turn they are taken. Cost 1 civil action to play, then discarded permanently.

Cards placed face-down on the future events deck as a political action. When revealed from the current events deck, they are evaluated immediately. Events may reward or punish players based on strength, culture, or other comparisons. Preparing an event scores culture equal to the card's level.

A special type of event. When revealed, triggers a colonization auction among all players. The winner sends military units to colonize and gains the territory as a colony with permanent effects and immediate benefits. Colonies can provide resources, population, tokens, and rating bonuses.

Military cards that organize units into armies with bonus tactical strength. Played for 1 military action during Action Phase. Stay exclusive for 1 round, then move to common tactics area. Copied by any player for 2 military actions. Age II and III tactics have normal and outdated army strength values.

Half-brown, half-green military cards with two uses: the top half provides defense bonus against aggressions, the bottom half provides colonization bonus. Played during colonization or defense, then discarded.

Full game only. Played as a political action targeting one opponent. Attacker spends military actions. Defender can play bonus cards and discard military cards for +1 each (up to military action total). If defender cannot match attacker's strength, the aggression succeeds and penalties apply.

Full game only. Declared as a political action, resolved at the start of the attacker's next turn. Both sides use only their cards in play, no bonus cards. Severity of penalties depends on the difference between the two sides' strengths. Either side can win.

Full game only, 3-4 players only. Offered as a political action to another player who can accept or refuse. Provides mutual benefits. Can be symmetric or asymmetric. Only 1 pact in your play area at a time. Cancelled by playing a new pact, using a political action, or age transition.

The first era covering BC and early centuries AD. Level 0 cards. Initial technologies are Age A. Age A ends when the starting player replenishes the card row for the first time. Age A leaders and unfinished wonders are removed when Age A cards become antiquated at the end of Age I.

The second era covering up to the 16th century. Level 1 cards. Begins when Age A ends. When the Age I civil deck runs out, Age A cards become antiquated, players discard them from hand, Age A leaders and unfinished wonders are removed, and each player loses 2 yellow tokens.

The third era covering up to the 19th century. Level 2 cards. Begins when Age I civil deck runs out. Age I cards become antiquated. Each player loses 2 more yellow tokens. In the simplified first game, Age II ending triggers the final round.

The final era covering the 20th century. Level 3 cards. Full game only. Introduces air force units and powerful wonders. Age III events determine final scoring. When Age III civil deck runs out, Age IV begins with no new decks, and the game ends after equal turns.
Age A leader. Does not directly change ratings but adjusts population increase rules, making it cheaper to grow your civilization. Excellent early-game leader for building a large workforce.
Age A leader. Gives an extra military action and increases strength rating by 1. Also provides options in the Politics Phase. Strong military-focused leader.
Age A leader. Provides culture-related bonuses. One of the leaders available in Antiquity for early culture production.
Age A leader. Provides science-related bonuses. Excellent for players pursuing a science-heavy strategy in the early game.
Age A leader. Facilitates building and wonder construction. Strong choice for players pursuing a wonder-centric strategy.
Age I leader. Provides significant military strength bonuses. Strong leader for military-focused strategies in the medieval period.
Age I leader. Provides bonuses related to building and technology development. Versatile leader supporting multiple strategies.
Age II leader. Powerful military leader providing strength and military action bonuses. Key leader for aggressive strategies in the Age of Exploration.
Starting government for all civilizations. Provides 4 civil actions, 2 military actions, and an urban building limit of 2 per type. Printed on the player board.
Age I government. Provides more military actions than Despotism. Good for military-focused strategies. Changed to via revolution or peaceful change.
Age I government. Provides 4 civil actions, 3 military actions, and bonuses to culture, strength, and happiness. Costs 6 science for peaceful change.
Age I government. Provides more civil actions, emphasizing economic and building focus over military. Good for wonder and technology strategies.
Age II government. Balanced government providing strong civil and military action totals with increased urban building limit.
Age II or III government. Emphasizes civil actions and economic power. May have restrictions on military aggression but provides strong culture and science capabilities.
Age A wonder. Built in 3 stages (costs 3, 2, 1 resources). When completed, provides an additional civil action. One of the most powerful early wonders.
Age A wonder. Provides military strength bonus when completed. Good for establishing early military dominance.
Age A wonder. Provides science-related bonuses when completed. Supports technology development strategies.
Age I or II wonder. Provides strength-related bonuses. Helps maintain defensive military posture without heavy investment in military units.
Age II wonder. Provides production-related bonuses, potentially including extra food or resource production. Supports economic strategies.

Urban building type (gray). Produces science points. Your starting technology Philosophy allows building level 0 labs. You begin with 1 lab producing 1 science. Higher-level lab technologies include Alchemy and Journalism.
Urban building type (gray). Produces culture points and happy faces. Your starting technology Religion allows building level 0 temples. Key for both culture production and preventing uprisings.
Urban building type (gray). Produces science points. Available from Age I onward. Provides an alternative or supplement to labs for science production.
Urban building type (gray). Produces culture points. Available from Age I onward. Key building for culture-focused strategies in the mid-to-late game.

Urban building type (gray). Produces culture points, happy faces, and contributes to strength rating. A versatile building type available from Age I onward that addresses multiple needs simultaneously.

Military unit type. Your starting Warriors card is level 0 infantry. Upgraded through Swordsmen (Age I), Riflemen (Age II), and Modern Infantry (Age III). The most basic and common military unit type.
Military unit type introduced in Age I. Provides strength per unit. Includes Knights (Age I) and other mounted units. Often featured in tactics card combinations with infantry.
Military unit type introduced in Age I or II. Provides high strength per unit. Includes Cannons and later Modern Artillery. Featured in advanced tactics card combinations.

Military unit type appearing only in Age III (full game). Contributes strength like other units but can also join any army to double its tactical strength. Each air force unit can join at most one army.

A set of military units matching the requirements of your current tactics card. Each army adds tactical strength to your strength rating. Multiple sets of matching units form multiple armies. Armies form automatically based on your tactic.
An army containing any unit that is 2 or more levels lower than the tactics card. Outdated armies receive the lesser of the two tactical strength values printed on Age II and III tactics cards.

The total strength sent to colonize a territory. Equals: unit strengths of sent units + army tactical strength (if units form armies) + colonization modifiers from cards in play + military bonus card values. Must equal or exceed your winning bid.
A bonus to your colonization force provided by certain special technologies (like Cartography) and other cards. Applies during colonization only, not to your regular strength rating.

A type of special (blue) technology that reduces the resource cost of urban buildings and allows building multiple wonder stages for a single civil action. Examples include Masonry and Architecture. Key technology for wonder-focused strategies.
A type of special (blue) technology that provides strength bonuses and military advantages. Enhances your military without requiring additional workers.
A special (blue) technology providing a colonization modifier. Makes your colonization forces more efficient, allowing you to win territory auctions with fewer sacrificed units.

An Age A action card that lets you build one stage of a wonder at a resource discount. A powerful early card for wonder-focused strategies. Only allows building one stage even if you have construction technology.

An action card that allows you to develop a technology at a discount. Can also be used to declare a revolution, paying all civil actions instead of paying 1 for the card. Only works when all civil actions are available.
One of two methods to change government. Costs 1 civil action plus the higher science cost on the government card. Lets you continue performing other actions on the same turn.

One of two methods to change government. Costs all civil actions (must all be unspent) plus the lower science cost. Any new civil actions gained are immediately spent without effect. Military actions unaffected.

The maximum number of buildings of one type you can have, determined by your government card. Despotism limits you to 2 per type. More advanced governments increase this limit. Applies only to urban buildings, not farms, mines, or military units.
The maximum number of civil cards you can hold in your hand, equal to your number of white tokens (civil action total). Does not prevent you from taking wonders, which go directly into play.

The maximum number of military cards you can hold, equal to your number of red tokens (military action total). Checked only during the discard step of your End-of-Turn Sequence. Drawing military cards at end of turn may temporarily exceed this limit.

Full game only. A player who is losing badly may resign as their political action. Wars against them are cancelled (but opponents score culture). The resigning player's civilization is removed from the game. Remaining players continue.
A numeric representation of a card's age. Age A = Level 0, Age I = Level 1, Age II = Level 2, Age III = Level 3. Used for determining upgrade costs, antiquation, and outdated armies.

Cards from two ages prior to the current age become antiquated at age transitions. Players discard antiquated cards from hand. Antiquated leaders, unfinished wonders, and pacts are removed from play. Completed wonders and developed technologies are never removed.
